coredata.dc:title: Rethinking lithium-ion battery management: Eliminating routine cell balancing enhances hazardous fault detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Hardy, John
Steggall, John
Hardy, Peter
coredata.dc:description: Current battery management systems for lithium-ion battery packs incorporate circuitry and software to carry out routine voltage balancing of cells in order to optimise battery performance by preventing the state of charge of individual cells drifting apart over time. Drawing on evidence from previous published research, this paper reports experimental results testing the hypothesis that routine balancing can be successfully eliminated over a battery's lifetime. The test method requires that specified steps are taken to prepare the cells and that a novel charging protocol is employed thereafter to ensure the pack is fully charged while accommodating cell-to-cell variations in capacity, aging and dynamic response. Extensive cycle testing on a range of different battery configurations found no evidence of loss of balance and hence of reduction in lifetime battery performance compared to conventional battery management systems incorporating routine dissipative balancing. Importantly, the experiments also found that elimination of routine balancing greatly enhances the scope for early detection of low-level self-discharge caused by developing internal short circuits. Internal short circuits are implicated in occasional highly destructive spontaneous battery thermal runaway events which have hitherto proved very difficult to predict when using conventional cell voltage balancing systems. The described method of battery management thus offers significant potential, especially for applications where the risk of sudden battery fires cannot be tolerated. In addition, the absence of high voltage balancing wires may bring other benefits, including simplified battery assembly, safer maintenance and reduced risk of hazardous wiring faults.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
Thermal runaway
Balancing
Internal short circuit
ISC
Soft short
Micro short circuit

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cell balancing and causes of cell drift – theory
Variations in charge current and load

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: 1 Test used pack level charge control often with early termination rather than a specific charge termination current
2 Each module consists of 4 cell blocks in one housing. Voltages are for a single cell block
3 Most cycles at 0.5 °C. Stages 5 and 6 and some capacity-checking cycles at lower rates
4 1500 cycles at 1C to 100 % DOD, 2000 cycles at 1C to 80 % DOD
5 ~250 cycles to 80 %, >500 to 70 % - interpolating graph on spec sheet
7 1C charge, 1C discharge, 500 cycles to 70 % of typical capacity, 1C charge, 2C discharge, 300 cycles to 80 % of typical capacity

1,2: R
ISC values are in turn 750 Ω, 1.5 kΩ and 3 kΩ; all cells in Pack A rebalanced when R
ISC removed.

3: R
ISC values are in turn 100 Ω, 40 Ω; only C6, C7 rebalanced when R
ISC removed

head.ce:title: Rethinking lithium-ion battery management: Eliminating routine cell balancing enhances hazardous fault detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Intercal (UK) Ltd., 24 Hartlebury Close, Redditch B98 9LY, UK
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Intercal (UK) Ltd., 24 Hartlebury Close, Redditch B98 9LY, UK

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Indra Renewable Technology Ltd., Unit 1, Sentinel House, Sparrowhawk Close, Enigma Business Park, Malvern, Worcestershire, WR14 1GL UK
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Indra Renewable Technology Ltd., Sparrowhawk Close, Malvern, Worcestershire WR14 1GL, UK

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Current battery management systems for lithium-ion battery packs incorporate circuitry and software to carry out routine voltage balancing of cells in order to optimise battery performance by preventing the state of charge of individual cells drifting apart over time. Drawing on evidence from previous published research, this paper reports experimental results testing the hypothesis that routine balancing can be successfully eliminated over a battery's lifetime. The test method requires that specified steps are taken to prepare the cells and that a novel charging protocol is employed thereafter to ensure the pack is fully charged while accommodating cell-to-cell variations in capacity, aging and dynamic response. Extensive cycle testing on a range of different battery configurations found no evidence of loss of balance and hence of reduction in lifetime battery performance compared to conventional battery management systems incorporating routine dissipative balancing. Importantly, the experiments also found that elimination of routine balancing greatly enhances the scope for early detection of low-level self-discharge caused by developing internal short circuits. Internal short circuits are implicated in occasional highly destructive spontaneous battery thermal runaway events which have hitherto proved very difficult to predict when using conventional cell voltage balancing systems. The described method of battery management thus offers significant potential, especially for applications where the risk of sudden battery fires cannot be tolerated. In addition, the absence of high voltage balancing wires may bring other benefits, including simplified battery assembly, safer maintenance and reduced risk of hazardous wiring faults.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para:
Since lithium-ion cells are very intolerant of overcharging or over-discharging, the current state of the art in battery management systems (BMS) specifies circuitry and control systems to monitor and equalise the state of charge (SoC) of individual cells or blocks of cells connected in parallel 1
1 In this paper, references to “cells” should be read as applying equally to blocks of cells in parallel where the context allows. to match the rest of the pack (“routine balancing”). Routine balancing is employed as a mechanism for preventing the state of charge of constituent cells diverging over time (“cell drift”), thus protecting cells from the risk of over-charge and/or over-discharge while ensuring that the optimum usable capacity of the pack is secured. This paper describes a programme of experiments which has tested the hypothesis that, with the use of suitable protocols, routine balancing can be eliminated while still maintaining optimum lifetime battery performance. Arising from these experiments, a potentially very powerful capability to detect very early-stage internal short circuits (ISC) has emerged and is investigated.

While the electrochemistry of lithium-ion cells is associated with exceptionally high coulombic efficiency (CE) and low self-discharge (SD) compared with other battery types, to date the consensus view among battery management engineers has been that in practice cell drift will still occur, materially limiting usable battery capacity unless routinely corrected. Commonly cited reasons for cell drift are the presence of intrinsic variations within batches of cells, notably in respect of capacity, internal resistance, self-discharge and response to aging, as well as extrinsic factors, including the effects of operating temperature [1–3]. To date, however, there has been little reported research into the precise causes of cell drift and only limited experimentation exploring the effect of long-term cycling without routine balancing. Indeed, a recently published review concluded that “despite the established use of balancing circuits, neither the true origin of voltage imbalance nor the benefits of cell balancing are as yet completely understood [4].

Against this background, the initial experiments described here are configured simply to measure cell drift in a range of lithium-ion battery types during prolonged cycling without the use of routine balancing. The results of these experiments were used to devise and test a method of battery management which eliminates routine balancing and applies a charging algorithm designed to maximise the battery's energy throughput while accommodating varying capacity, impedance and aging characteristics of individual cells. In the final experiment, the performance of this method is compared with a conventional BMS operating under identical conditions in terms of energy capacity, temperature, charge times and degradation rates over 1000 cycles to 70 % depth of discharge (DoD).

Over the course of the experiments, it became evident in that cell drift in healthy cells was negligible under the conditions tested. Critically, however, it was noted that the occasional incidences of cell drift that did occur were all associated with emerging cell faults. These may include early-stage ISC which are recognised to be the cause of occasional apparently spontaneous battery fires in lithium-ion battery systems. An ISC, typically arising as a result of manufacturing defects or inappropriate operating conditions, can evolve over time with very gradual localised temperature increases ultimately causing separator melting and a risk of thermal runaway (TR) and fire [5–7]. Early-stage ISCs remain difficult to detect, despite the application of much research effort [8,9]. Following the notorious Boeing 787 “Dreamliner” incidents in 2013 [10,11], a number of major fires have impacted the battery energy storage sector [12,13], while similar risks linked to electric vehicle (EV) battery faults have led to vehicle recalls by several manufacturers [14,15].

The remainder of this paper is organised as follows. Section 2 provides a theoretical background describing the possible root causes of cell drift and reviewing previous reported research into battery cycling without routine balancing. Section 3 outlines the programme of experiments carried out to explore the scope for specifying a functioning BMS effectively without routine balancing and in addition to examine the potential for very sensitive ISC detection. Section 4 presents and discusses the results of these experiments. Section 5 sets out conclusions and proposals for further research.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell balancing and causes of cell drift – theory

ce:section.ce:para: The simplest routine balancing method employed in commercial BMS is “dissipative” (or “passive”), wherein excess charge is bled from cells with the highest estimated SoC until all cells in the pack have an equal estimated SoC. An alternative approach, “redistributive” (or “active”) balancing, involves shunting charge between cells with the aim of achieving better energy efficiency and increased usable pack capacity. The latter method can be effective under some conditions, but the required circuitry adds complexity and cost [2], with the result that most commercial applications still favour the simpler and more reliable dissipative method [16]. With dissipative balancing, the usable capacity of the battery is limited by the capacity of the weakest cell, so if that cell is always optimally charged and it also reaches full discharge first then no increase in performance can be realised by further balancing efforts.

The variously cited reasons for the occurrence of cell drift over the life of a battery can all be analysed in the context of three possible unbalancing mechanisms, namely cell-to-cell differences in SD, CE or imposed charge currents or loads. Variations in SD mean that over time some cells in a series string require additional coulombic input to achieve full charge. Variations in CE mean that a given coulombic input adds less charge to some cells than others. Cell-to-cell variations in charge currents or loads from any cause have a direct impact.

When considering both CE and SD, it is important to note that a standard design feature of lithium-ion cells, the so-called “anode overhang”, can in certain circumstances cause significant temporary cell drift. Depending on the charge history and SoC of a cell, lithium gradually diffuses into or out of the electrochemically inert overhang area, causing high or low apparent CE and SD which can continue over some hundreds of hours [17]. A batch of cells cycled in series having previously been stored at varying SoC would thus exhibit cell drift for a significant period. The extent of the anode overhang effect in some battery configurations has been shown to be substantial [18] and it is possible that a lack of full consideration of the issue has contributed to previous erroneous assumptions about the extent and variability of SD and CE, and hence the likelihood of cell drift in lithium-ion batteries.

Although SD in healthy lithium-ion cells is widely acknowledged to be low, there has until recently been only limited published data of sufficient accuracy and detail to quantify its long-term effect on cell drift. One study, however, published in 2010, set out the results of a test on four 1.4 Amp-hour (Ah) Sony 18650-HC cells which were separately charged to between 3.6 V and 4.1 V and monitored while in an open circuit state at 23 °C over a period of 7.5 years. SD in three of the cells was between 4.51 and 8.91 μA, with one high outlier which the authors speculate may have had a low level ISC. SD rates in the three other cells positively correlated with increasing open circuit voltage. Another test of the same cell type, which involved holding a series string at a mean cell float voltage of 3.95–4.05 V for 7.5 years, resulted in cumulative cell drift of <3 mV, suggesting that long term cell-to-cell variation in SD would not be a significant cause of imbalance in a battery pack of these cells [19].

A later study has reported on simulations applying a once-daily charge-discharge cycle to a series string of three 1.25 Ah cells over a 30-day period. Even with high temperature gradients (0 – 30 °C) within the string, the impact of reversible SD on imbalance was judged to be at a low level that would not appear to warrant a need for balancing systems. The researchers acknowledged, however, that the time period studied was too short for irreversible aging related losses and other possible unbalancing factors to be taken into account [20].

A recent study involving very accurate measurement of SD in 3.5 Ah nickel-rich silicon-graphite LG Chem INR18650-MJ1 cells has reported average reversible SD figures of between 2 μA and 4.2 μA at 25 °C [21]. Voltage decay modelling for the same cell type under conditions simulating operation in both temperate and tropical environments showed largely negligible voltage drift [22]. The researchers concluded from this that different self-discharge rates in the cell format studied are unlikely to be the main reason for voltage drift and hence the need for balancing. A follow-up report describes a simulation, based on a typical load cycle of an EV with the same cells in a 168s20p configuration, which was run for 2920 partial cycles of 24 h, each cycle being to a DoD of 13.7 %. The simulation, which included imposed temperature gradients between each 20p cell block within the series string, provided a comparison between dissipative balancing and cycling with no balancing. The results showed no more than 1 % improvement in performance in the pack with balancing. Since this simulation did not involve deep discharge, the possibility of increasing cell drift with greater degradation and non-linear aging was not ruled out [23].

In contrast to other common battery chemistries, the CE of lithium-ion cells is extremely high. CE of 0.9998–0.9999 has been reported for lithium cobalt oxide cells during cycling at C/10 between 2.8 and 4.075 V for three years at 55 °C [24]. An average CE of 0.9999 was found in an 18650HP-Fe Lithium iron phosphate (LFP) cell, measured over around 2000 full charge cycles at 45 °C, where early cycling showed a slightly impaired CE as the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer formed, after which very high CE was maintained until the cells reached an advanced stage of degradation [25]. The same study reported that three 18650CD nickel manganese cobalt (NMC) cells, cycled under similar conditions, exhibited a two-stage degradation process. Extremely high CE levels were observed, continuing for some 800 full charge cycles until a late-stage inflection point between 80 % and 70 % normalised capacity where rapid degradation took place, coinciding with an accelerating loss of CE to 0.998 and below. The authors note the contrast between the aging behaviour of the LFP and NMC cells, associating this with different degradation mechanisms. It has separately been observed that this second-stage degradation phase showing declining CE in NMC cells may be linked to the onset of lithium plating at the anode, a process that in turn enhances the risk of TR events [26,27].

Important conclusions emerging from the above studies were that it is aging processes alone that cause the CE of healthy lithium-ion cells to fall below 1.000 [24], and consequently that a fall in CE signifies accelerating degradation [25]. The consequence for battery management is that, if a batch of healthy cells is cycled under generally similar conditions, CE-induced cell drift will be directly linked to cell degradation. While cells from the same production batch may not have identical electrochemical properties and may therefore age differently [28,29], and inhomogeneous operating conditions may also affect degradation rates, it remains the case that the most degraded cell will provide the limiting capacity of the pack, assuming dissipative balancing is applied. It is therefore reasonable to infer that variations in CE rates sufficient to cause material cell drift and usable capacity loss may not arise during the usable life of a lithium-ion battery, at least until the onset of very late-stage degradation processes close to end-of-life. However, given the incomplete state of current knowledge concerning the various degradation processes and their interplay [30] and the associated challenges of modelling [31] this can only be tested through experimentation, as described in Section 3 below.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Variations in charge current and load
ce:section.ce:para: In addition to SD and CE effects, cell drift will naturally arise in a series-connected battery pack if there is any unintended variation in charge currents and loads imposed on each cell over the life of the battery. Such variations can for example arise from the voltage monitoring system, cell level BMS electronics powered by the cell or cell-level current draw for ancillary equipment. The degree of cell-to-cell variation in charge current or load that can be tolerated without causing material imbalance over time is in part a function of the capacity of the cell and the design life of the battery. As an example, a variation of only 0.1mΑ in a 100 Ah battery would cause nearly 1 % loss of capacity per year.

While most sources of cell-to-cell variation in charge current and load can be designed out of a battery system, it remains the case that the voltage response of individual cells in a series-connected pack is bound to vary owing to differences in capacity, internal resistance and cell kinetics [28]. In the absence of routine balancing there is therefore a challenge in achieving full charge of the battery while ensuring individual cell voltages remain within acceptable limits. This requires consideration of the charging algorithm, which is discussed in Section 3.3 below.

A series of eight laboratory tests was carried out between 2012 and 2020 to explore the scope for effective management of series-connected lithium-ion batteries without routine balancing. These were grouped into three phases:
•
Phase I: Two initial simple experiments testing for cell drift over extended cycling. The tests used standard commercial smart chargers.
•
Phase II: Based on these initial results, Tests 3–7 evaluated the performance of a range of battery chemistries. As well as checking further for cell drift, these experiments evaluated the effectiveness of a novel charging algorithm designed to eliminate the need for balancing currents, as described in Section 3.3 [32]. Test 7 was designed to assess the effect of severe degradation caused by a sustained imposed temperature gradient.
•
Phase III: A final large-scale experiment, Test 8, was carried out to further test the performance of this method of battery management. The experiment was configured with a three-fold purpose. Firstly, it compared baseline performance of a conventional dissipative balancing BMS alongside the proposed method of battery management, in terms of usable capacity, energy throughput and overall charging time. Secondly, it provided data to enable the scope for early stage ISC detection to be explored in greater detail. Thirdly, it provided data to support the parameterisation of an equivalent cell model, which in turn supported virtual simulation of ISCs in scenarios that would be difficult to reproduce experimentally (to be reported in a future paper).

Raw data collection for the purposes of analysis included current flows at pack level, whole pack voltage and individual cell voltages. Positive and negative terminal temperatures for all cells were recorded from Test 3 on. Readings were taken every 30 seconds for Tests 1–7 and once per second for Test 8.

Charge rates ranged between 0.5C and 1.8C. Discharge was typically to 70 % DoD. This broadly mirrors many real-world applications that may not routinely experience full discharge. From Test 2 onwards the packs were periodically fully discharged to 100 % DoD to provide a means both of checking for cell drift and tracking the irreversible loss of pack capacity as the cells aged. With the exception of Tests 7 and 8, temperature chambers were not used, allowing ambient temperatures to fluctuate within the range of 7–22 °C, broadly reflecting seasonal temperate-zone conditions. While most tests were halted by cell failure or on reaching the rated cycle life of the cells, several were extended beyond their rated cycle life.

In the absence of routine balancing, special attention must be paid to equalising the SoC of cells prior to assembly. Voltage is an effective proxy for cell SoC provided there is sufficient rest period after the end of current flow to allow cell voltage to stabilise. In principle voltage equalising can be carried out at any SoC, but since lithium-ion cells typically exhibit a greatly increased change in voltage with state of charge (δV/δQ) approaching 100 % DoD (“the knee”), accurate SoC equalisation can most readily be achieved at or near 100 % DoD (“bottom balancing”). For these experiments a simple electronic balancer was fabricated, comprising a resistor and a MOSFET transistor coupled with a microcontroller. The cells were each discharged through the resistor until the target voltage was reached. Thereafter the microcontroller was configured to switch the resistor in and out (initially very rapidly) to maintain cell voltage at the target value.

The time required to stabilise the voltage varied with cell type but typically 15 to 30 minutes was sufficient since the tests used new cells which had been supplied at approximately equal SoC. As described in Section 2.1, the effect of anode overhang would need to be taken into account if using cells with varying charge histories.
Table 1 is a summary of the tests. Where stated, cell chemistries and cycle lives were as described by the manufacturer or supplier and have not been independently verified.

These initial experiments employed LFP cells in an 8s1p configuration. Two key parameters were set:
•
The SoC of the constituent cells was accurately equalised prior to assembly using the bottom balancing protocol described in Section 3.1.
•
The design topology of the test rigs was specified so as to ensure that all cell-to-cell variations in charge currents or loads were maintained at low μA levels, calculated to ensure they could cause no material imbalance over the period of the experiment.
The experiments used series-connected test rigs comprising eight cell modules. Charging followed a modified CC/CV protocol based on whole pack voltage.
Phase II tests were all carried out on packs comprising 18650 cylindrical cells of various battery chemistries with the same 8s1p configuration and set-up parameters as for Phase I tests.

As described in Section 4.1 below, it had been observed during the Phase I experiments that the cell voltages tended to remain close throughout most of the charge/discharge cycle except at the very end of charge as the current was tapered to maintain whole battery voltage. Typically, the voltage of one or more cells would exceed their target, with other cell voltages falling away. In the absence of routine balancing, this is to be expected as a result of variations in cell capacity, impedance or degradation rates, leading to sub-optimal charging of the pack and a risk of over-charging individual cells.

To address this, an experimental charging protocol was introduced, denoted here as “Constant Current/Constant Cell Voltage” (CC/CCeV). Once the standard CC phase is completed, the CCeV phase modulates the charging current applied to the whole battery so as to sustain the highest voltage cell at a pre-defined limit, thus maximising its safe charging rate, as follows:
•
Step 1 - CC phase: Charge the battery at constant current until the first cell reaches a predetermined limiting voltage (V
limit ). The cell is tagged as “cell-max”; move to Step 2.
•
Step 2 - CCeV phase: Modulate charging current to the battery to a level that maintains cell-max at V
limit . The charge is terminated when the charge current falls to a specified cut-off point, for example 5 % of the original charging current in the CC phase, at a point where cell-max is deemed to be fully charged.

The identity of the cell-max may change during the CCeV phase as a consequence of the varying dynamic response of individual cells. By applying this procedure as the charging voltage is tapered, no cell is allowed to go over its maximum rated voltage at any time, and charge termination is reached when a cell attains V
limit at the cut-off charge current, corresponding to that cell reaching 100 % SoC. At this point, although not all cells are at 100 % SoC, pack coulombic capacity is maximised since all cells will reach full discharge together. In contrast, a pack charged conventionally with dissipative balancing will bring all cells to 100 % SoC but must cease discharge as soon as the weakest cell reaches minimum voltage.

The set-up for each of Tests 3–6 was essentially similar, comprising pack cycling at room temperature and checking periodically for cell drift. Test 7 comprised a period of normal cycling at room temperature after which a temperature gradient was introduced.

After testing and development of the CC/CCeV charging algorithm, Test 8 was configured to enable long term performance comparison with a conventional dissipative balancing BMS, including measures of usable capacity and overall charging times.

The test rig comprised two packs, each composed of two 4s/2p Nissan Leaf modules connected in series, functionally equivalent to an 8 series pack of 115 Ah. The two packs were cycled alongside each other under identical conditions. One pack (Pack A) was prepared and cycled as for Tests 3–7, applying the CC/CCeV protocol. This test introduced more sophisticated hardware and software than had been used in Phases I and II, which permitted more accurate measurements of voltages, current and cell temperatures. The second (Pack B) was managed by a conventional balancing BMS, the Orion “BMS 2” manufactured by Ewert Energy Systems. After a period of test cycles, three test sequences were initiated with the introduction of simulated ISC of different resistances to selected cells in each pack.

After these test sequences were complete, the two packs were operated normally for several hundred cycles in order to compare baseline performance both at the outset and as the cells aged. The key performance measures recorded were overall charging time, coulombic capacity and energy throughput.

Evaluation of the exploratory Phase 1 experiments was largely limited to assessing whether repeated cycling of an initially well-balanced pack led to material cell drift over time. Test 2, which continued for over 2000 cycles, enabled a secondary measure to be applied, namely the rate of loss of pack coulombic capacity compared to the rated loss of cell capacity as specified by the manufacturer.

An additional test criterion introduced for Phase 2 was whether the CC/CCeV charge algorithm would consistently ensure that the weakest cell would be fully charged without any cell in the pack being charged above its rated voltage, thus ensuring that the pack (so long as it remained balanced) would achieve no less usable coulombic capacity than the theoretical maximum achievable with a dissipative balancing BMS.

Phase 3 Test 8 allowed relative coulombic capacities, energy capacity, charge times and cell degradation rates of the two battery management methods to be measured alongside each other under identical conditions.

Assessment of cell drift by voltage measurement needs to take into account the voltage imbalances that can arise for reasons other than SoC imbalance. Voltages may diverge when current is flowing as a result of cell-to-cell impedance variations, while the rate at which cell voltages stabilise after current flow ceases can vary between cells [33]. Both of these effects can be simulated by the use of Equivalent Circuit Models [34]. Direct reading of the terminal voltage of a cell is, however, a generally reliable proxy for state of charge if no current is flowing and sufficient time has elapsed since the cessation of current flow for all cell voltages to stabilise. For these experiments, a post-charge rest period (PCR) and a post discharge rest period (PDR), each of at least 15 minutes, was programmed into the software that controlled cycling. The choice of 15 minutes rest is a compromise between timely execution of the experiments and ensuring an adequate period for voltage stabilisation. Under certain circumstances (notably for example when reversible lithium plating occurs in a degraded cell) full voltage stabilisation is known to require a much longer rest period [35].

Voltage spread at the end of PCR was adopted as the primary metric for assessing cell drift. This was partly because the chosen cycle pattern involved discharge variously to 70 % and 100 % DoD, with the result that PDR voltage spread measurement was affected by the greatly increased change in voltage with state of charge approaching 100 % DoD. An additional benefit of using PCR voltage is that, since many commercial BMS routinely balance at the top of charge, this enables direct comparison with these systems.

In comparing PCR voltage data, it is normal to observe a spread from the outset when the pack has been bottom-balanced, reflecting any small differences in capacity between nominally identical cells [28]. The key metric for assessing cell drift, therefore, is the trend in the spread of PCR voltages over time, rather than the absolute value of the spread.

At the end of most of the tests, estimated remaining usable pack capacity is given. Note that manufacturers' rated cell life data typically relates to cycling to a specified voltage corresponding to about 100 % DoD, compared with generally 70 % DoD used in these tests, and temperatures and applied currents may also differ. It is also commonly noted that usable pack capacity loss during aging is greater than rated single-cell capacity loss [36].

As described below, non-uniform behaviour was observed in a number of individual cells during testing, arising from a range of causes. These behaviours may variously be observed at PCR or PDR but also under charge or discharge. Trends in both PDR voltage and end of discharge (EoD) voltage spread can provide useful additional information for those cycles where DoD is approaching 100 %, since as previously noted the very large δV/δQ beyond the knee provides the most sensitive measure of SoC. Considering PCR, PDR and EoD voltage data in combination can provide useful insights into cell behaviour under certain conditions.

Presentation of the test results is principally graphical, since this provides a simple and effective way of both monitoring cell drift and identifying anomalous behaviour by individual cells. This method is supplemented by numerical data where helpful to assist interpretation, notably in Tests 7 and 8.

As noted above and described in detail in Section 4, the results from Test 1 onwards gave an indication that anomalous cell behaviour exhibiting as drift in one or more cells in a pack may be indicative of emerging cell faults, potentially providing an early warning of emerging hazards that a conventional BMS would suppress through routine cell balancing. Where they occur, these anomalous behaviours are directly observable from voltage records. It is not within the scope of this paper to develop this important issue further than to point the way toward possible algorithms that would enable a BMS to flag issues when they emerge, thus giving early warning before they evolve into sudden battery failure or thermal events.

The initial cell behaviour for Test 1 is illustrated at Fig. 1 , where solid lines represent cell voltages (l.h. axis) and the red pecked line pack current (r.h. axis). During CC charging the voltages are aligned but as the whole pack approaches limiting voltage and the charger starts to taper the current, there is a marked divergence of cell voltages until charging terminates. After charging ceases, the voltages start to converge during PCR. Cell voltages fully converge at the start of discharge, remaining aligned throughout discharge and in the PDR period. This pattern was maintained as Test 1 progressed, confirming that cell drift was not occurring.

By Cycle 500, an anomaly was observable in Cell 6 which was starting to lose capacity more rapidly than the other cells, which all remained closely balanced. This progressed until Cycle 575 (Fig. 2 ) when the affected cell was close to failure and the test was terminated. By this point, the cell was only accepting much-reduced charge, thus severely limiting usable pack capacity. Note that Cell 6 has the highest voltage on charge and the lowest voltage during discharge, confirming that the issue was a cell fault rather than loss of balance.

This experiment and the observed behaviour of Cell 6 gave the first indication that eliminating routine balancing might open opportunities for very early detection of emerging cell faults, including ISC. The precise cause of the accelerated loss of capacity of Cell 6 was not identified.

This test used larger prismatic cells, applying the same “whole pack” CC/CV charge profile as for Test 1. The rig was tested over a 16-month period, completing 2008 cycles to 70 % DoD. The response of these cells showed a similar voltage divergence approaching full charge but on full discharge there was no discernible cell drift, as illustrated in Fig. 3 (Cycle 100) and Fig. 4 (Cycle 1900) which show the cell voltages going over the knee almost together.

At Cycle 100, the voltage spread after a 19-minute PCR period was 21 mV. This spread remained virtually unchanged throughout the test and was measured at 19 mV after 1900 cycles. At the conclusion of the experiment, the cells were tested individually and were found to have remaining capacity of between 33.30 and 34.61 Ah, or 75 % to 80 % of nominal. There was a significant correlation between irreversible cell capacity loss and the observed voltage spread at EoD, with the three lowest capacity cells (Cells 5,7 and 8) reaching EoD first and the highest capacity cell (Cell 4) projected to reach it last. The loss of pack capacity as the pack aged, measured by coulomb counting a full discharge every 50 cycles, is illustrated in Fig. 5 . The results are consistent with the manufacturers' rated cycle life for an individual cell of 2000 cycles at 80 % DoD.
It is reasonable to infer from these data that the absence of routine balancing has not led to cell drift or any loss of usable coulombic throughput.

This test provided the first experimental trial of the CC/CCeV charge algorithm described in Section 3. It also introduced a new chemistry, lithium cobalt oxide (LCO). When compared with the LFP cells in Tests 1 and 2, this cell type exhibits a more modest relaxation in voltages during PCR. The change in pack voltage with changing SoC is also more marked but the knee remains as a marker of approaching EoD.

Cell 1 failed prematurely, resulting in termination of the test after less than half the expected cell cycle life. Examination of the voltage traces in Fig. 6 and Fig. 7 track the cell's final stages of deterioration alongside the remaining cells, which all stay in balance. A critical point was reached from Cycle 142. Although Cell 1 was reaching V
limit first it became reasonably closely aligned by the end of CCeV charging and during the PCR. Two cycles later, however (Cycle 144), it was still reaching V
limit first but voltage fell away from the remaining cells as current reduced during CCeV in response to another cell reaching V
limit . This pattern, including the continuing relative voltage decline during PCR, becomes more marked during the final few cycles to failure (Fig. 7). The precise cause of the cell failure was not investigated.

The relatively short test duration and the early deterioration of Cell 1 prevented any meaningful analysis of capacity loss from the remaining seven healthy cells; however, they showed no evidence of cell drift.

Owing to the premature failure of one cell in Test 3, Test 4 was run using an identical test pack. On this occasion, one otherwise healthy and stable cell was observed to have raised impedance from the outset. This fortuitously provided the opportunity to test the effectiveness of the CCeV algorithm in handling significant cell-to-cell impedance variation. Test 4 completed 659 cycles, well beyond these cells' rated cycle life. As shown in Fig. 8 , the high impedance Cell 1 caused a voltage response under charge which triggered the V
limit transition to CCeV early. Thereafter, however, charging was accommodated by a more gradual reduction in current during the CCeV phase, modulated at a pace which held the high impedance cell at V
limit . The voltages of all eight cells converged towards the end of CCeV and became fully aligned at PCR, confirming that the outlier cell was exhibiting high impedance and not loss of balance. This result demonstrates that the CCeV charging algorithm can compensate for a mismatch in cell internal resistance, avoiding any loss of usable capacity.

All cells in Test 4 remained stable throughout the 659 cycles (Fig. 8 and Fig. 9 ) with no observable cell drift. Pack capacity at cycle 500 in the test was 1632mAh. This compares with the rated cell capacity of new cells of 2200mAh, reducing to 70 % (1540mAh) after 500 full cycles.
The test ended when Cell 8 suddenly failed open circuit during Cycle 319. The remaining six cells had again sustained close balance throughout the test, with PCR and PDR voltages remaining aligned.

This test pack was identical to that used in Test 6 but was configured to assess the effect of temperature gradients, which were expected to impair the dynamic performance of the colder cells and induce more rapid degradation. The test began with 98 cycles at room temperature. Cells 5–8 were then placed in a simple refrigerated enclosure while Cells 1–4 remained at room temperature (Fig. 14 ). The disposition of the cells within the refrigerated enclosure was such that cell warming during charge and discharge would affect the cells differently, Cells 6 and 7 on the inside of the pack being up to 2 °C warmer and subjected to a lesser temperature gradient than Cells 5 and 8 on the outside.

While routine balancing of PCR voltages at this late stage might therefore provide modest additional usable capacity, the evidence suggests this would only occur very shortly before the pack reached the end of its useful life. Although the cause of the rapid degradation after Cycle 300 was not investigated, one explanation may be accelerated lithium plating at the anode, which can be induced by a combination of low temperature and faster charging [27,35] and is associated with heightened risk of TR either as a result of dendritic growth causing ISC [38,39], or by initiating exothermic reactions with the electrolyte [35,40].

In this “parallel running” test, each cycle of the two packs consisted of a full charge at a constant current during the CC phase followed by the CCeV phase for Pack A and the CV phase for Pack B, then a short PCR period, then discharge followed by a PDR period.

CC charging was 56A (approximately 0.5C) and V
limit was set to 4.15 V for both packs. The Orion BMS in Pack B uses a proprietary variant of a conventional CC/CV algorithm and was configured to carry out dissipative balancing at currents of up to approximately 200 mA per cell whenever cell potentials were above 3900 mV and the voltage spread of the pack exceeded 10 mV. To ensure controlled environmental conditions, both packs were installed within an LMS Series 1A Model 80 thermal chamber. Charge and discharge were interleaved so that the same power supply and load were used for both battery packs, facilitating comparison of results.

Discharge patterns were varied through the test to include a mix of simple constant current together with variable current sequences using discharge patterns adapted from the New European Driving Cycle (NEDC) and Worldwide harmonised Light vehicle Test Procedure (WLTP). Discharge on these cycles was to approximately 70 % DoD. In all stages of testing, on at least every 20th cycle, a constant current discharge was used to 100 % DoD (2.7 V) to measure available pack capacity. Table 2 sets out details of the test stages.

Monitoring data for this experiment was enhanced to a frequency of once per second. These data allowed key performance indicators, notably total charging times, the coulombic throughput of each pack and the extent of cell drift, to be measured and compared over a large number of cycles. The total test duration was 10 months with a total of 1004 cycles.

The potential for early ISC detection through cell voltages was investigated for each BMS by interrupting cycling and placing fixed resistors (R
ISC ) across individual cell terminals [7]. This was implemented in three test stages (10, 12 and 14). At the conclusion of each of these three stages, the R
ISC were removed. After Test Stages 10 and 12, Pack A was rebalanced before normal cycling resumed in order to eliminate the unbalancing effect of the R
ISC . After Test Stage 14, which used much lower resistance R
ISC and induced greater imbalance in the affected cells 6 and 7, only these two cells were rebalanced. No rebalancing was carried out in Pack B, since the Orion BMS fully restores any loss of balance automatically in the cycles after the R
ISC are removed.

Cell drift in both packs was measured at the end of PCR (Fig. 21 ), at the points indicated by the dashed circles. Since Pack A used bottom balanced cells, there was some PCR spread from the outset reflecting small cell capacity variations.

Fig. 22 and Fig. 23 illustrate the voltage at the end of PCR of the two packs through Test Stage 12, when R
ISC of 750 Ω, 1.5 kΩ and 3 kΩ respectively were fitted to three cells in each pack. As expected, the lack of balancing in Pack A (Fig. 22) has resulted in clearly detectable voltage drift in the three R
ISC cells, proportionate to the ISC currents which were approximately C/2400, C/3200 and C/9600 respectively. With these very low ISC currents, however, the conventional BMS in Pack B (Fig. 23) clears the small voltage drift as a result of its routine balancing function and as a result no detectable anomaly emerges, even over sustained cycling.

For Test Stage 15, on completion of the three ISC-detection simulation test stages, the two packs were subjected to continued cycling in order to compare the baseline performance of the two battery management systems as the cells aged. No PCR cell drift was detectable in Pack A throughout the 500+ cycles of stage 15 (Fig. 24 ) where all voltages remain within approximately a 20 mV band throughout.

Capacity trends were measured by coulomb-counting on the full charge cycles, using current data provided by the power supply in order to eliminate current measurement discrepancies introduced by differences in calibration. The coulomb count was zeroed at the bottom of discharge and then accumulated up to the end of charge, as identified by dashed circles in Fig. 25 . Although the two BMSs controlled charging based on their own independent measures of cell voltage, pack voltage was also monitored using the load and power supply. Comparison of coulombic capacity trends were made from cycles 500 to 1004 in Test Stage 15, after allowing several cycles for Pack B to rebalance following removal of the simulated ISC at the end of Test Stage 14. As illustrated in Fig. 26
and Table 3 , the discharge capacity of the two packs remained very close, with both rigs showing a steady reduction through aging as cycling continued. Pack A with the CC/CCeV algorithm showed slightly higher capacity throughout, though this may simply be owing to the natural capacity variation which is known to occur during cell manufacture [28].

An early tapering of Pack A coulombic capacity compared with Pack B can be observed from Cycle 500 which gradually attenuated over subsequent cycles. From an analysis of EoD voltages it was evident that Cells 6 and 7 in Pack A were showing some cell drift after rebalancing which steadily declined and was negligible by Cycle 760. Since the mean SoC of Cells 6 and 7 had been substantially reduced by the insertion of low-resistance ISC during Test Stage 14, this finding is likely to be due to the anode overhang effect described in Section 2.1 [17,18].

Energy throughput of the two packs is set out in Table 3, from which it can be seen that Pack A again marginally exceeds Pack B. As with coulombic capacity, this difference may be due to capacity variation of the cells.

An additional metric relevant to many applications is the speed of charging. In all Stages, the maximum charge duration for Pack A with the CC/CCeV algorithm was slightly shorter than for Pack B (Table 3). An explanation for this is that, as configured in these tests, the Orion BMS managing Pack B initiates the tapering of charge current shortly before any cell in the pack reaches Vlimit. By comparison the CC/CCeV algorithm maintains full charging current up to the moment where cell-max reaches Vlimit at which point current is immediately reduced to the required level. This has the effect of shortening the overall charging time slightly while still ensuring that all cells remain within Vlimit.

Overall, it can be seen that the baseline performance of the BMS method under test compared favourably with a state-of-the-art balancing BMS under the conditions tested. While any single trial of capacity retention is susceptible to the effect of variation in the properties of the cells in the packs, there was no evidence that either pack contained cells that were outliers on impedance or on degradation rate. This experiment has also confirmed the indications that had emerged during several of the earlier tests, namely that eliminating routine balancing is an effective enabler of very early cell fault detection, whereas routine balancing by a conventional BMS has the effect of masking the fault.

Routine cell voltage balancing is an established feature of current lithium-ion battery management systems. In this paper, we report the results of a programme of experiments using a novel method of battery management that eliminates all routine balancing after first assembly by exploiting the extremely high coulombic efficiency and negligible self-discharge of healthy lithium-ion cells.

Eight laboratory experiments and trials were undertaken on test packs covering a range of battery chemistries and cell types, operating temperatures, charge and discharge rates and degradation states, all without applying routine cell voltage balancing after assembly. Several of the tests were continued up to and beyond the cells' rated cycle life.

A novel model-free charging protocol is presented as a means of optimising charge of the weakest cell and hence the usable capacity of a battery, whilst keeping all its constituent cells within safe maximum voltage and accommodating cell-to-cell variations in impedance, capacity and degradation rates. Although experimentation showed that cell-to-cell differences can emerge during the aging process after repeated cycling, this novel charging method was shown to fully accommodate cell-to-cell variations in internal resistance and charge capacity, thus ensuring that pack capacity is maximised.

The final test directly compared performance of this approach to battery management with a conventional dissipative voltage balancing system, providing evidence of fully comparable capacity over sustained cycling coupled with a marginal saving in charging times. These results were maintained throughout over 1000 charge/discharge cycles to 70 % depth of discharge. This and other testing presented herein supports the hypothesis that routine balancing is not a necessary feature of a fully effective battery management system.

Given the diversity of cell types and test conditions in the various experiments, statistical analysis of the results was not considered appropriate. However, with 64 cells tested and over 6000 charge cycles completed, it is clear that the consistent results observed are strongly indicative of the potential of the approach presented, and a sound basis for undertaking further research.

An important finding arising from the investigations and simulations was that anomalous voltage drift, when it occurred, was found to be diagnostic of an emerging cell fault which could subsequently be tracked through to eventual failure.

This was further explored in the final test which showed that elimination of routine balancing provides new opportunities to detect extremely low levels of self-discharge of the kind associated with potentially hazardous ISC in their earliest stages.

A separate report is in preparation, further analysing the Test 8 ISC simulations combined with additional modelling, with the aim of developing an algorithm with low computational power which can reliably detect and distinguish very early-stage internal short circuits before they reach a critical stage of potential thermal runaway.

Work is also planned to explore further whether any material loss of balance may occur in heavily degraded cells after reaching their rated end of life, and to compare near end-of-life performance with a conventional BMS.

Subject to the findings of this further research, the proposed method of battery management and fault detection described here appears to offer a solution to the long-standing and hitherto intractable problem of detecting the early warning signs leading to sudden thermal runaway in lithium-ion batteries. Additional benefits may arise, at least in some applications, from the elimination of separate high voltage balancing wires to each cell, thus enabling safer maintenance while eliminating a potential source of hazard from electrical faults.

While system cost will vary according to the type of application, production volumes and regulatory compliance requirements, initial appraisal suggests there may be scope for savings compared to a conventional BMS owing to the reduced hardware and software requirements and simplified pack assembly. These aspects are currently being investigated further.

John Hardy: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision. John Steggall: Software, Resources, Data curation. Peter Hardy: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

John Hardy has patent #ZL 201580038973.7 issued to Intercal (UK) Ltd. John Hardy has patent #EP3170240A pending to Intercal (UK) Ltd. John Hardy has patent #GB2528350 issued to Intercal (UK) Ltd. John Hardy has patent #US10707686B issued to Intercal (UK) Ltd.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effects of rest time after Li plating on safety behavior - ARC tests with commercial high-energy 18650 Li-ion cells
Electrochim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: [35] T. Waldmann and M. Wolfhart-Mehrens, “Effects of rest time after Li plating on safety behavior - ARC tests with commercial high-energy 18650 Li-ion cells,” Electrochimica Acta, vol. 230, pp. 454-460, 2017, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2017.02.036.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Understanding aging mechanisms in lithium-ion battery packs: from cell capacity loss to pack capacity evolution
J. Power Sources

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: [36] Y. Zheng, M. Ouyang, L. Lu and J. Li, “Understanding aging mechanisms in lithium-ion battery packs: from cell capacity loss to pack capacity evolution,” J. Power Sources, vol. 278, pp. 287-295, 2015, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpowsour.2014.12.105.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lithium-ion battery discharge behaviors at low temperatures and cell-to-cell uniformity
J. Automot.Saf.Energy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: [37] J. Zhang, J. Huang, L. Chen and Z. Li, “Lithium-ion battery discharge behaviors at low temperatures and cell-to-cell uniformity,” J. Automotive Safety and Energy, vol. 5, pp. 391-400, 2014, http://dx.doi.org/10.3969/j.issn.1674-8484.2014.04.013.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lithium Plating in Lithium-Ion Cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: [38] A. H. Zimmerman and M. V. Quinzio, “Lithium Plating in Lithium-Ion Cells,” 16-18 November 2010. [Online]. Available: https://www.nasa.gov/sites/default/files/atoms/files/1-lithium_plating_azimmerman.pdf. [Accessed 18 November 2021].

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lithium plating detection and quantification in Li-ion cells from degradation behaviors
ECS Trans.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: [39] Y. Zhang, X. Li, L. Su, Z. Li, B. Liaw and J. Zhang, “Lithium Plating Detection and Quantification in Li-Ion Cells from Degradation Behaviors,” ECS Trans, vol. 75, pp. 37-50, 2017, https://doi.org/10.1149/07523.0037ecst.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Thermal runaway triggered by lithium plating at the Anoda after fast charging
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces
